name: The Last First Memory
description: Elder Senn carries one memory that is not in the main recitation. A specific memory — too specific to survive generalization, too fragile to include in a recitation that drifts four percent per generation. She has decided to tell it once.
accept_text: She waits until the others have dispersed from the circle — not long, the Discarded are efficient about ending as they are about everything. When you're alone with the small fire she looks at you directly. 'You stayed through the full recitation.' She says it as a fact. 'Most visitors leave by the second hour. The full recitation is — it is not exciting. It is not meant to be exciting. It is meant to be complete.' She pauses. 'I have a memory that is not in the recitation. I have carried it for fifty years. My grandmother gave it to me on the last night I sat with her, separately from the circle, just the two of us, because she said: this one is too specific for the circle but too important to lose.' She looks at the fire. 'Specific memories are dangerous. They change in the telling. Every time you say a specific thing, the specific thing shifts slightly toward what you believe it should be rather than what it was. I have told this memory to no one in fifty years because I did not want to corrupt it.' She meets your eyes. 'But I am ninety-four. And you were here. And you understand what the Wastes were before the war, or as much of it as anyone from the surface understands.' A pause. 'Come back tomorrow night. I will tell it. One more night to make sure I have it exactly right. One more night to sit with whether telling it is the right thing.' She pauses. 'I think it is. I have thought so for ten years. It is time.'
complete_text: She tells it exactly as she told you she would: once, slowly, with the care of someone who has been holding something for fifty years and has decided the holding is over. The harvest sound. The crystal frequency in the soil. The Aetherian traders who came down from the sky for three weeks a year just to stand in the passes and hear the plains hum. Her great-great-grandmother with four children running north into the mountains when the bombs started falling, carrying the sound of the harvest in her memory because it was not heavy and did not need to be put down. When she finishes she sits quietly. The fire is very low. 'I have told it exactly right,' she says. 'Or as exactly right as a fifty-year-old memory of a memory of a memory can be told. Four removes from the original.' She looks at the ash. 'My grandmother was the third remove. I have been the fourth for fifty years.' She looks at you. 'You are the fifth. If you tell it — tell it exactly. Not the meaning of it. Not what you think it says about the war or the Wastes or what was lost. The sound itself. The three weeks. The sky-ships holding position above the plains.' She pauses. 'Specific memories survive by being told exactly. The moment you generalize it into what it means, it stops being the memory and becomes the meaning, and the memory is gone.' She picks up a coal from the edge of the fire and holds it in her palm for a moment — the Discarded work with heat in ways that look uncomfortable and apparently aren't. 'The Aetherians came down every year for the sound. Three hundred years ago there was a sound that made them want to come down.' She lets that sit. 'I don't know what to do with that except carry it. I have been carrying it for fifty years.' She passes the coal back to the fire. 'Now you carry it too. The weight is the same. It doesn't get lighter when you share it. But the number of people who could drop it gets smaller.'
